questions.text: A guy is standing in a lift falling freely under gravity and releases a ball from his hand. As seen by the ball, the boy:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When the lift is falling freely under gravity, both the guy and the ball inside the lift are experiencing the same acceleration due to gravity. Therefore, from the perspective of the ball, the guy appears to be stationary relative to it. This is because they both fall at the same rate and there is no relative motion between them within the falling lift.

When the lift is falling freely under gravity, both the guy and the ball inside the lift are experiencing the same acceleration due to gravity. Therefore, from the perspective of the ball, the guy appears to be stationary relative to it. This is because they both fall at the same rate and there is no relative motion between them within the falling lift.

When the lift is falling freely under gravity, both the guy and the ball inside the lift are experiencing the same acceleration due to gravity. Therefore, from the perspective of the ball, the guy appears to be stationary relative to it. This is because they both fall at the same rate and there is no relative motion between them within the falling lift.

When the lift is falling freely under gravity, both the guy and the ball inside the lift are experiencing the same acceleration due to gravity. Therefore, from the perspective of the ball, the guy appears to be stationary relative to it. This is because they both fall at the same rate and there is no relative motion between them within the falling lift.

questions.explanation: The acceleration of the body does not depend on the mass (for freely falling). Hence both boy and ball have same acceleration. Hence their velocities at every point would be same. So "Relative" velocity is 0. Hence the boy will remain stationary as seen by the ball.

questions.text: The radius of the moon is 27% off the earth’s radius and its mass is 1.2% of the earth's mass. Find the acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the moon:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: et M & M' be the mass of earth & moon. also, R & R' be their radius respectively. given is; M' 1.2% M = 0.012 M R= 27% R = 0.27 R Gravitational acceleration on earth's surface is; g = GM/R2 Gravitational acceleration on supposed planet is; g'= GM'/R2 g'= G (0.012M)/ (0.27R)2 g'= (0.012/0.272) GM/R^2 g'= 0.1646 g g'= 0.1646 x 9.8 g = 1.613 m/s² Therefore, acceleration due to gravity on the moon's surface is 1.613 m/s²

questions.explanation: Let M & M' be the mass of earth & moon. Also R & R' be their radius respectively. Given is - M' = 1.2% M = 0.012 M R' = 27% R = 0.27 R Gravitationalacceleration on earth's surface is - g = GM / R2 Gravitationalacceleration on supposed planet is - g' = GM' / R'2 g' = G(0.012M) /(0.27R)2 g' = (0.012/0.272)GM/R^2 g' = 0.1646 g g' = 0.1646 × 9.8 g' = 1.613 m/s2 Therefore, acceleration due to gravity on the moon's surface is 1.613 m/s2.

questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Right answer. In space, it is possible to create "artificial gravity" by spinning your spacecraft or space station. When the station spins, centrifugal force acts to pull the inhabitants to the outside. This process could be used to simulate gravity.
Wrong answer.

questions.explanation: In space, it is possible to create "artificial gravity" by spinning your spacecraft or space station. Technically, rotation produces the same effect as gravity because it produces a force (called the centrifugal force) just like gravity produces a force.

questions.text: If the diameter of the earth becomes two times its present value and its mass remains unchanged, then how would the weight of an object on the surface of the earth be affected?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If the diameter of the Earth becomes two times its present value, the distance from the center of the Earth to its surface would also double. According to the law of universal gravitation, the gravitational force between two objects is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. Therefore, if the distance between the object and the center of the Earth doubles, the gravitational force experienced by the object would be reduced to one-fourth of its original value, not doubled.
Hence the correct option is B.

This option is not correct because the weight of an object is directly proportional to the mass of the Earth and inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the center of the Earth. If the diameter of the Earth doubles, the distance from the center of the Earth to the surface would also double. This doubling of distance would result in a reduction in the gravitational force experienced by the object to one-fourth of its original value, not one-third.

This option is not correct because the weight of an object on the surface of the Earth is determined by the gravitational force exerted by the Earth on the object. If the diameter of the Earth doubles, the distance between the object and the center of the Earth also doubles. As a result, the gravitational force experienced by the object would be reduced to one-fourth of its original value, not remain the same.

This option is not correct because if the diameter of the Earth doubles, the distance from the center of the Earth to the surface would also double. According to the law of universal gravitation, the gravitational force between two objects is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. Therefore, if the distance between the object and the center of the Earth doubles, the gravitational force experienced by the object would be reduced to one-fourth of its original value, not halved.

questions.explanation: Let M be the mass of earth and R be the radius, we know, g=GM/R2 so, if diameter is doubled i.e. d' = 2d which means R' = d = 2R ⇒ g' = GM/4R2 = g/4 So g becomes g/4. Also Weight=Mass of the object W'=(mass of object*g)/4 That is, the weight becomes 1/4.

questions.text: When a body moves in the direction of gravitational force i.e. towards the earth, the work is done by the force of gravity on the body and is _, whereas when the body moves against the direction of gravitational force, the corresponding work done is _.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When a body moves in the direction of the gravitational force, the work done by the force of gravity is indeed negative. This is because the displacement of the body is in the same direction as the force, resulting in negative work.
When the displacement is in the direction of force, work done is positive and when displacement is in the opposite direction of the force, work done is negative.

If the body moves against the direction of the gravitational force, the work done by gravity is negative, not positive. The force and displacement are in opposite directions, resulting in negative work.

If the body moves against the direction of the gravitational force, the work done by gravity is negative. However, when the body moves in the direction of the gravitational force, the work done by gravity is also negative, not positive. The force and displacement are in the same direction, resulting in negative work.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: When the work is done in the direction of the force then work done is said to be positive. However, if the work is in opposition to the direction of force then the work done is said to be negative.

questions.text: A solar day is the time interval between two successive appearances of the sun overhead. The time that is referred to the rotation of the earth about its axis is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A solar day is time lnterval between two successlve appearance of the sun overhead. Thus mean second, representing the basic unit of time, was originally defned as (1/60) (1/60) (1/24) of a mean solar day. The tme that is referred to rotation of the earth about its axis is called universal time.

The length of the day refers to the time it takes for the Earth to complete one full rotation about its axis. It is the specific time interval associated with the rotation of the Earth. The length of the day is commonly defined as 24 hours, which corresponds to the time it takes for the Sun to appear overhead twice (from noon to noon). Therefore, option (b) is not the most accurate term for the time interval related to the rotation of the Earth about its axis

The term "time interval" is a general term referring to a specific duration or duration between two events. While the concept of a time interval can be applied to various contexts, it is not specifically used to describe the time interval related to the rotation of the Earth about its axis. Therefore, option (c) is not the most accurate term for this context

questions.text: Find the gravitational force of attraction between two balls each weighing 10 kg, when placed at a distance of 1 meter apart.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: F = (G * m1 * m2) / r^2 where F is the gravitational force, G is the gravitationalconstant (approximately 6.674 × 10^-11 N m^2/kg^2), m1 and m2 are the masses ofthe objects and r is the distance between their centers. Given that both balls weigh 10 kg and are placed 1 meter apart,we can substitute the values into the formula: F = (6.674 × 10^-11 N m^2/kg^2 * 10 kg * 10 kg) / (1 m)^2 F = (6.674 × 10^-11 N m^2/kg^2 * 100 kg^2) / 1 m^2 F = 6.674 × 10^-9 N Therefore, the gravitational force of attraction between thetwo balls weighing 10 kg each, when placed 1 meter apart, is approximately6.674 × 10-9 Newtons.
As per calculations above, 6.674 × 10-9 Newtons is the correct answer and rest are incorrect.
As per calculations above, 6.674 × 10-9 Newtons is the correct answer and rest are incorrect.

As per calculations above, 6.674 × 10-9 Newtons is the correct answer and rest are incorrect.

questions.explanation: F = (G * m1 * m2) / r2 where F is the gravitational force, G is the gravitationalconstant (approximately 6.674 × 10-11 N m2/kg2), m1 and m2 are the masses ofthe objects and r is the distance between their centers. Given that both balls weigh 10 kg and are placed 1 meter apart,we can substitute the values into the formula: F = (6.674 × 10-11 N m2/kg2 * 10 kg * 10 kg) / (1 m)2 F = (6.674 × 10-11 N m2/kg2 * 100 kg2) / 1 m2 F = 6.674 × 10-9 N Therefore, the gravitational force of attraction between thetwo balls weighing 10 kg each, when placed 1 meter apart, is approximately6.674 × 10-9 Newtons.

questions.text: In motion of satellites, necessary centripetal force is provided by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Gravitational force is an attractive force. It provides the centripetal force, directed towards the centre of the circle, producing circular motion. This is needed to keep a satellite in orbit around a planet. Coloumb's force is not present, since both the Earth and the satellite are uncharged. Magnetic force is also absent as there is no moving charge. The nuclear force is a force that acts between the protons and neutrons of atoms and binds them together in a nucleus. It is a weak force and therefore can not provide the centripetal force.

questions.explanation: Satellites revolve around planets because the gravitational force of attraction between the satellites and the planets acts as a centripetal force for them. This is why option A is correct. Other forces either don’t exist or are too weak to act as a centripetal force.

questions.text: For a satellite so that it could stay over the same point on Earth's surface the orbital radius r is given by _.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: . The correct formula for the orbital radius (r) of a satellite in a geostationary orbit is: r = (G * M * T^2 / (4 * π^2))^(1/3) Where: G is the gravitational constant (approximately 6.67430 x 10^-11 m^3/(kg·s^2)) M is the mass of the Earth (approximately 5.972 x 10^24 kg) T is the orbital period of the satellite (in seconds) π is a mathematical constant approximately equal to 3.14159 By using this formula and plugging in the appropriate values, you can calculate the orbital radius (r) required for a satellite to stay over the same point on Earth's surface.

questions.explanation: For a satellite, the gravitational force provides the centripetal force; therefore: Fg=Fc {where Fg is the gravitational force and Fc is the centripetal force} GMm/r2=mv2/r Make R the subject of the equation to obtain: r=[GMT2/4x2]⅓

questions.text: If M and m represent the mass of Earth and mass of a satellite respectively; then the orbital speed v of satellite in a circular orbit of radius r around Earth will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In a circular orbit, the gravitational force on the satellite provides the necessary centripetal force to keep the satellite moving in a circle. The gravitational force between Earth and the satellite is given by: F = G(Mm)/r2 where G is the universal gravitational constant. Since the gravitational force provides the centripetal force, we have: F = mv2/r Equating the above two equations, we get: G(Mm)/r2 = mv2/r Simplifying this equation, we get: v2 = GM/r Therefore, the speed of a satellite in a circular orbit of radius r around Earth is given by v2 = GM/r.
This option is incorrect. It includes the square root (√) operation on the right-hand side, which is not consistent with the correct formula for orbital speed.

This option is incorrect. It includes the square root (√) operation on the right-hand side, but the order of the terms r and GM is incorrect. The correct formula should be v^2 = GM/r, not v^2 = √r/GM.
This option is incorrect. It includes the square root (√) operation and the term 2GM, but the order of the terms is incorrect. The correct formula for orbital speed is v^2 = GM/r, not v^2 = √(2GM/r).
This option is incorrect. It includes the square root (√) operation and the term 2r, but the order of the terms is incorrect. The correct formula for orbital speed is v^2 = GM/r, not v^2 = √(2r/GM).

questions.explanation: In a circular orbit, the gravitational force on the satellite provides the necessary centripetal force to keep the satellite moving in a circle. The gravitational force between Earth and the satellite is given by: F = G(Mm)/r2 where G is the universal gravitational constant. Since the gravitational force provides the centripetal force, we have: F = mv2/r Equating the above two equations, we get: G(Mm)/r2 = mv2/r Simplifying this equation, we get: v2 = GM/r Therefore, the speed of a satellite in a circular orbit of radius r around Earth is given by v2 = GM/r.

questions.text: An object of mass ‘m’ is suspended in an elevator moving downward with acceleration equal to acceleration due to gravity. What is the apparent weight of object?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The apparent weight of the object is the force exerted on it by the elevator floor, which is equal to the net force acting on the object.Using the formula Fnet = mg - ma Where,m is the mass of the object g is the acceleration due to gravity a is the acceleration of the elevatorIn this case, the elevator is moving downward with an acceleration equal to the acceleration due to gravity, which means a = g.Therefore,Fnet = mg - ma = m(g - g) = 0.So, the apparent weight of the object is zero. Option A is the correct answer.

This option is incorrect because if the elevator is moving downward with an acceleration equal to the acceleration due to gravity, the apparent weight of the object will not be twice its actual weight. The apparent weight will be less than the actual weight, not greater.

This option is incorrect because when the elevator is moving downward with an acceleration equal to the acceleration due to gravity, the apparent weight of the object will be zero. The object will experience a sensation of weightlessness, as if it's in freefall.
This option is incorrect because, as mentioned earlier, the apparent weight will be zero, not half of the object's actual weight. The object will effectively be weightless in the elevator.

Option A is the correct answer.

options.explanation: As per the explanation, this option is incorrect.

If the elevator falls freely, the downward acceleration of the man in the elevator is: a = g Now, the force exerted by the floor on the man is equal to the actual weight of the man minus the net upward force on him. F = ma - mg F = mg - mg F = 0 N Hence, the force exerted by the floor on the man is zero.

questions.text: The gravitational field strength on the surface of the Earth is g. The gravitational field strength on the surface of a planet of twice the radius and the same density is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: As per the solution, this option is incorrect.

Now, the gravitational field strength (g') on the surface of the new planet will be determined by the following equation: g' = G * (M' / R'²) where: G is the gravitational constant (a universal constant, approximately 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N(m/kg)²), M' is the mass of the planet (which is 8 times the mass of the Earth), R' is the radius of the planet (twice the radius of the Earth). Since the mass and radius are both larger by a factor of 8 and 2, respectively, the gravitational field strength on the surface of the new planet (g') will be: g' = 8g/4 = 2g Therefore, the gravitational field strength on the surface of a planet with twice the radius and the same density as the Earth will be two times the gravitational field strength on the surface of the Earth (2g).
This is the incorrect option.
This is the incorrect option.

questions.explanation: The unit "per ohm second" (Ω⁻¹s) is indeed equivalent to the unit "farad" (F), which is the SI unit of capacitance. Both units represent different aspects of electrical properties. In the context of capacitance, the farad (F) measures the ability of a capacitor to store an electric charge for a given voltage. It's defined as one coulomb of charge per volt of potential difference. On the other hand, "per ohm second" (Ω⁻¹s) represents the unit of electrical conductance multiplied by time. Conductance is the reciprocal of resistance (measured in ohms), and when multiplied by time (seconds), it becomes equivalent to capacitance (farads). In mathematical terms: 1 F = 1 Ω⁻¹s This relationship highlights the interconnectedness of various electrical properties and units. Now, the gravitational field strength (g') on the surface of the new planet will be determined by the following equation: g' = G * (M' / R'²) where: G is the gravitational constant (a universal constant, approximately 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N(m/kg)²), M' is the mass of the planet (which is 8 times the mass of the Earth), R' is the radius of the planet (twice the radius of the Earth). Since the mass and radius are both larger by a factor of 8 and 2, respectively, the gravitational field strength on the surface of the new planet (g') will be: g' = 8g/4 = 2g Therefore, the gravitational field strength on the surface of a planet with twice the radius and the same density as the Earth will be two times the gravitational field strength on the surface of the Earth (2g). questions.text: A satellite is moving in a circular orbit at a constant speed due to gravity.Which of the following statements is correct about the above figure?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The earth does zero work on the satellite.
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. In a circular orbit, the gravitational force between the earth and the satellite is perpendicular to the displacement of the satellite. In this case, work is defined as the dot product of force and displacement. Since the dot product of two perpendicular vectors is zero, the work done by the earth on the satellite is zero.

options.text: The earth does positive work on the satellite.
options.explanation: This is incorrect. As mentioned earlier, the gravitational force between the earth and the satellite is perpendicular to the displacement of the satellite. Positive work is done when the force and displacement are in the same direction. Since the force and displacement are perpendicular, the work done by the earth on the satellite is not positive.

options.text: The earth does negative work on the satellite.
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Similar to the previous explanation, since the force and displacement are perpendicular, the work done by the earth on the satellite is not negative.

options.text: The concept of work is not applicable in circular motion.
options.explanation: This is incorrect. The concept of work is applicable in circular motion. Work is defined as the dot product of force and displacement. Although the work done by the earth on the satellite is zero in this case, it doesn't mean that the concept of work is not applicable.
